senses.definition: to be the first person or team that obtains an advantage over a competitor in any contest

examples.x: Despite their best efforts, our team wasn't able to draw first blood in the game and fell behind early.
During the negotiations, the union was able to gain the upper hand when they drew first blood by presenting a compelling argument for their demands.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡˌoʊ wˈʌn bˈɛɾɚ ðɐn ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to excel in doing something compared to anyone else who has ever tried it

examples.x: The company's latest smartphone model goes one better than its predecessor with a more advanced camera system.

senses.definition: to defeat someone soundly or thoroughly in a competition or argument, often with the implication that the opponent had no chance of winning

examples.x: During the chess tournament, Jane was confident in her skills, but her opponent ended up cleaning her clock with a series of surprising moves.

senses.definition: superior to others in terms of advancement, achieving success, or modernity

examples.x: The fashion designer's bold new collection was ahead of the curve, featuring styles and colors that were completely unexpected and ahead of their time.
The tech company's innovative new product is so far ahead of the curve that competitors will struggle to catch up.

word_list.pronunciation: blˈoʊ ðə dˈoːɹz ˈɔf ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to achieve a significantly higher level of success compared to someone or something else

word_list.pronunciation: ɡɛt hæv ðə bˈɛst bˈɛɾɚ ʌv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to be able to beat someone or something due to being superior in terms of skill or strength

examples.x: Although she was one of the few women in her field, Rachel had the best of her peers.
Despite facing numerous problems in his career, Tom finally had the best of his setbacks.
Despite feeling overwhelmed by the sheer volume of material she needed to learn, Jenna was determined to get the best of her exams.
The boxer was determined to win the fight, but his opponent's superior speed and agility got the best of him.

senses.definition: to manage to invent something that improves upon an existing concept or object

examples.x: The musician built a better mousetrap by incorporating new sounds and genres into their music, resulting in a more diverse and experimental sound.
The software company was determined to build a better mousetrap than its competitors, with a new product that was more user-friendly and had better features.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ðə ɔːɹ ɐn ˈɛdʒ ˌoʊvɚɹ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to be in a slightly better position when competing against someone else

examples.x: The experienced soccer team has been practicing hard and has an edge over their opponents in the upcoming match.
The experienced team has the edge over their less-experienced opponents and is expected to win the game.
The new smartphone had the edge over the last product, with its larger screen and faster processor.
The online retailer had an edge over the other services with its faster shipping and wider selection of products.

word_list.pronunciation: nˈɑːk ɔːɹ hˈɪt ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ˌaʊɾəv ðə pˈɑːɹk

senses.definition: to achieve significantly better results compared to other people or things

examples.x: The athlete's record-breaking performance in the championship game knocked his opponents out of the park, securing his place in history and earning him widespread admiration and respect.
The basketball player's impressive shooting skills were hitting opponents out of the park, scoring multiple three-point shots and leading his team to a victory.
The company's latest advertising campaign was a huge success, hitting it out of the park with record-breaking sales and customer engagement.
The musician's new album is a masterpiece, with every song knocking it out of the park.

word_list.pronunciation: lˈiːv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɪnðə dˈʌst

senses.definition: to defeat or outperform someone or something with ease

examples.x: The popular new social media platform left its rivals in the dust, attracting millions of users and revolutionizing the way people connect and share information online.
The video gamer is leaving his opponent in the dust with his expert knowledge of the game mechanics and quick reflexes.

senses.definition: to be much better, quicker, more successful etc. than someone or something else

examples.x: The dancer's performance was so mesmerizing that she left her rivals standing and won the competition with ease.
The new product design was so innovative that it left the competition standing, quickly dominating the market and securing a commanding lead.

senses.definition: used to refer to someone who is slightly better in terms of being prepared, skilled, etc. when compared to someone or something else

examples.x: The experienced hiker knew the trail well and was always one jump ahead, anticipating obstacles and finding the best path forward.

senses.definition: to increase one's level of effort, skill, or performance in a particular activity or area to achieve better results or compete more effectively

examples.x: If you want to pass your next math test, you need to step up your game and study more.
The musician stepped up his game and practiced his instrument for hours every day, resulting in a more polished and professional sound.

word_list.pronunciation: biː stɹˈiːts ɐhˈɛd ʌv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to deliver a much better performance compared to that of others'

examples.x: The experienced lawyer is streets ahead of her young colleagues, with a deep understanding of the law and years of courtroom experience.
The new smartphone is streets ahead of its predecessor, with a larger screen and more powerful processor.

senses.definition: to do something before someone else does it
a decisive victory in which a team or player achieves consecutive wins in any game, contest, or other similar events

examples.x: The political party achieved a clean sweep of the election, winning all the seats in the parliament and forming a majority government.
The tennis player achieved a clean sweep of her matches, winning every set and advancing to the finals.

senses.definition: to win an argument or other competitive situation

examples.x: The athlete trained hard and was able to come out on top in the competition, winning the gold medal.

senses.definition: to achieve success or victory in a particular situation or task

examples.x: The determined athlete carried the day, overcoming adversity and winning the race with a strong finish.
The employee worked hard to complete all their tasks and win the day, impressing their boss with their productivity.

senses.definition: a person or thing that is ahead of others in a race or other competitive situation

examples.x: The candidate who is leading in the polls is considered the front runner in the upcoming election.

word_list.word: [snatch] victory from the jaws of defeat
word_list.pronunciation: snˈætʃ vˈɪktɚɹi fɹʌmðə dʒˈɔːz ʌv dɪfˈiːt

senses.definition: to achieve a victory or success despite initially seeming unlikely or impossible, particularly after experiencing a setback or failure

examples.x: The family was lost in the wilderness without any food or water, but they managed to find a way out and snatched victory from the jaws of defeat by surviving.
The soccer team was losing the game 2-0 at halftime, but they managed to score three goals in the second half and snatch victory from the jaws of defeat.

senses.definition: used to refer to a victory that is achieved only by a small margin

examples.x: The car manufacturer's latest model outperformed its competitors by a nose, offering slightly better fuel efficiency and a more comfortable ride.
The singer won the talent competition by a nose, impressing the judges with her powerful voice and stage presence.

senses.definition: to be in an advantageous position when competing against someone else

examples.x: The basketball team holds the aces with their great players, teamwork, and strong coach, so they are likely to win the game.
The negotiator held all the aces with their persuasive arguments, information, and bargaining power, and got a favorable deal.
The student had the aces going into the exam, but they didn't study enough and ended up doing poorly.
The team that has the best players, the best strategy, and the most resources has all the aces in the game.
